compatibility_statement: Compatible with Oracle Health's (formerly Cerner Millennium) published FHIR R4 CapabilityStatement across both host fronts: fhir-ehr.cerner.com (provider/EHR-launch tier) and fhir-myrecord.cerner.com (patient standalone-launch tier). Same backend, same tenant IDs, different access scopes. Not affiliated with or endorsed by Oracle Corporation. Trademarks of Oracle Corporation and former Cerner Corporation are used for identification only.

access_scope_notes.description: Oracle Health publishes the same Millennium backend behind two host fronts. Provider-launch traffic goes through fhir-ehr.cerner.com and includes administrative resources (Account, Basic, ChargeItem, Communication, Group); patient standalone-launch traffic goes through fhir-myrecord.cerner.com with those resources omitted. Clinical-resource shape (element-level cernerisms in element_deviations, scope syntax, token endpoint, OperationOutcome shape) is identical across scopes. Source: tier_divergence diff of 1448-endpoint provider modal cluster (cerner-cluster-A) against 1356-endpoint patient modal cluster (cerner-patient-cluster-A), validated 2026-04-28.

verification.source_quote: Diff computed 2026-04-28 from 1358 patient-tier CapabilityStatements harvested under tests/golden/production-fleet/cerner-patient/2026-04-28/ against the provider-tier modal cluster in ehrs/cerner/production_fleet.json.

phase_b_findings.description: Editorial summary of consequential behaviors observed during Phase B against Cerner's sandbox (open + secure tiers, 2026-04-27).

headlines.finding: Oracle Health's 'System Account' application class is a confidential client with a shared secret — not the asymmetric JWT-bearer flow that SMART Backend Services Authorization 1.0.0 prescribes for system clients
headlines.evidence: Registration through code-console.cerner.com under the 'System Account' application class issues a `client_id` and `client_secret` pair. The token endpoint at `https://authorization.cerner.com/tenants/<tenant-id>/protocols/oauth2/profiles/smart-v1/token` accepts the `client_credentials` grant only with HTTP Basic authentication (i.e., `Authorization: Basic <base64(client_id:client_secret)>`). Submitting `private_key_jwt` (RS384) with a `client_assertion` resolvable through a JWKS URL is rejected with `error=invalid_client` / 'invalid-signature' — the registration form has no JWKS URL field for this class, so Oracle Health never fetches the published JWKS. The `smart-v1` segment in the token URL path suggests an asymmetric `smart-v2` variant may exist on a different endpoint, but it is not advertised on this registration class.
headlines.integrator_impact: Epic's Backend Services flow is asymmetric only (`private_key_jwt` with no shared secret); Oracle Health's 'System Account' is symmetric-secret instead. An integrator porting an Epic Backend Services client to Oracle Health cannot reuse the JWKS-publishing pattern and must instead store and rotate a long-lived shared secret. This is a real divergence from SMART Backend Services Authorization 1.0.0 §3.2, which mandates asymmetric authentication for system clients.

citations.label: Oracle Health Millennium FHIR R4 — App Registration (System Account)

headlines.finding: Oracle Health requires SMART v2 scope syntax (`.r` / `.rs`) at the token endpoint — `.read` is rejected with empty-scopes
headlines.evidence: Probing eight scope-syntax variants at the token endpoint (identical client credentials, identical tenant) produces a clean v1-vs-v2 split. `system/Patient.read` (SMART App Launch v1 syntax) returns HTTP 400 `{"error":"invalid_scope","error_uri":"https://authorization.cerner.com/errors/urn%3Acerner%3Aerror%3Aauthorization-server%3Aoauth2%3Atoken%3Aempty-scopes/instances/..."}` — the URL-decoded URN `urn:cerner:error:authorization-server:oauth2:token:empty-scopes` is the vendor error code (captured 2026-05-02 in `tests/golden/cerner/phase-b-2026-04-27/error-token-empty-scopes-v1-syntax-2026-05-02.json`). `system/Patient.r` (SMART App Launch v2 read action) returns HTTP 200 with an access_token. Patient search additionally requires the `.rs` (read + search) action — `system/Patient.r` allows `GET /Patient/{id}` but `GET /Patient?...` returns HTTP 403 `insufficient_scope` until the scope is upgraded to `system/Patient.rs`.
headlines.integrator_impact: Epic accepts SMART v1 scope syntax (`system/Patient.read`); Oracle Health requires SMART v2 (`.r`, `.rs`, `.rsu`). Cross-vendor clients must send the correct syntax per EHR or risk silent empty-scopes and `insufficient_scope` failures. The `.r` vs `.rs` distinction also catches v1 clients that conflate read-by-id with search; both are needed when the integrator expects to call both.

citations.label: SMART App Launch STU 2.2 § Scopes and launch context (v2 syntax)

headlines.finding: Oracle Health's secure-tier authorization is enforced per-resource and may diverge from the scopes printed in the issued token
headlines.evidence: A token issued with the scope set `Patient.rs Observation.rs Encounter.rs AllergyIntolerance.rs Condition.rs MedicationRequest.rs Procedure.rs Immunization.rs` was probed against the secure tier (`fhir-ehr.cerner.com`) using the canonical sandbox patient. `GET /Observation?patient={pid}&category=laboratory` returns HTTP 200 with lab observations. `GET /Condition?patient={pid}`, `GET /AllergyIntolerance?patient={pid}`, `GET /Patient/{pid}`, and `GET /Patient?name=smart` all return HTTP 403 'Forbidden resource'. Same token, same tenant, same patient — only the resource type changes. The vendor portal therefore maintains a per-resource allowlist that overrides the OAuth scope grant.
headlines.integrator_impact: Receiving a token with `system/Condition.rs` does not guarantee the application can read Conditions on the secure tier. Integrators must verify access per-resource at runtime rather than trust the token's scope claim. This is an Oracle-Health-side authorization layer that is not surfaced through OAuth metadata or the SMART configuration document.

headlines.finding: Oracle Health's secure-tier Patient search rejects unconstrained queries — at least one of `_id`, `identifier`, `name`, `family`, `given`, `birthdate`, `phone`, `email`, `address-postalcode`, or `-pageContext` is required
headlines.evidence: `GET /Patient?_count=5` against the secure tier returns HTTP 400 with an OperationOutcome diagnostic: 'at least one of _id, identifier, name, family, given, birthdate, phone, email, address-postalcode, or -pageContext must be provided.' The required-search-param set is enforced even with system-level scopes.
headlines.integrator_impact: Population-level patient sweeps (e.g. `Patient?_count=200` to enumerate test patients) do not work on Oracle Health's secure tier. Enumeration must instead be driven through Bulk Data Access (`$export`) or through the open tier (`fhir-open.cerner.com`), which has no such constraint. This breaks the catalog-the-sandbox pattern that works against Epic's public sandbox.

citations.label: FHIR R4 — Patient resource search parameters

headlines.finding: Oracle Health publishes a two-tier public sandbox (open + secure) — Phase B measurement can run partially without any registration
headlines.evidence: `fhir-open.cerner.com/r4/<tenant-id>` requires no Authorization header and returns deidentified data: `GET /Patient/{pid}` → HTTP 200 with the documented sandbox demographics; `GET /Patient?name=smart` → HTTP 200 with ten patient entries; `GET /Observation?patient={pid}&category=laboratory` → HTTP 200 with lab observations. The same paths against `fhir-ehr.cerner.com` require Bearer authentication and may return 403 per the per-resource finding above. The open-tier CapabilityStatement is shorter than the secure-tier CapabilityStatement (1,883 lines vs ~1,500+ lines, ~61 KB) — these are independent deployments behind a shared tenant identifier.
headlines.integrator_impact: Among the major EHRs measured so far, this two-tier open/secure split is unique to Oracle Health. Epic gates every FHIR resource behind OAuth. The open tier is invaluable for offline measurement (Phase B can partly run without app registration), but it is dangerous to depend on operationally — production Oracle Health customers do not expose this tier, so sandbox-shaped code that relies on no-auth reads will fail in production.

verification.source_quote: See ehrs/cerner/CapabilityStatement.json + auth_overlay below for verbatim evidence; secure-tier Phase B golden fixtures pending.

auth_overlay.verified_scopes: system/Patient.rs system/Observation.rs system/Encounter.rs system/AllergyIntolerance.rs system/Condition.rs system/MedicationRequest.rs system/Procedure.rs system/Immunization.rs
auth_overlay.notes: Oracle Health's 'System Account' flow uses HTTP Basic with a shared secret rather than the JWKS-based JWT-bearer flow Epic offers. The token endpoint accepts `client_credentials` only with `client_secret_basic`. Scopes follow SMART App Launch v2 syntax (`.r`/`.rs`/`.rsu`); v1 `.read` is rejected with empty-scopes. A portal-side authorization layer overrides OAuth scope claims per-resource: a token granted with `system/Condition.rs` may still receive HTTP 403 on a Condition read. Two sandbox tiers exist: `fhir-open.cerner.com` (no auth, deidentified) and `fhir-ehr.cerner.com` (Bearer-required, with the per-resource allowlist). The vendor portal exposes no JWKS URL field on this client class, so any published JWKS is not consulted by the token endpoint.

verification.source_quote: POST grant_type=client_credentials scope='system/Patient.rs ...' Authorization: Basic <b64(id:secret)> -> HTTP 200 access_token (859 chars); GET secure /Observation?...&category=laboratory -> 200; GET secure /Condition?... -> 403 forbidden; GET open /Patient/12724066 -> 200
